senses.definition: The process of deciding how to share and give resources, such as money, time, or space, to different people or purposes.
examples: The committee is responsible for the allocation of funds to local projects.
Efficient allocation of resources is essential for economic growth.

senses.definition: A specific amount of money, time, or other resource that is officially given to someone for a particular purpose.
examples: We have already used our entire travel allocation for this year.
Each department receives a fixed allocation for training.

senses.definition: The number of new company shares that are officially given to each buyer when shares are first sold.
examples: Each investor was informed of their share allocation by email.
Small investors complained that their allocation of shares was too low.

senses.definition: Plural of allocation: separate amounts of money, time, or other resources that are officially given to different people or purposes.
examples: Budget allocations for education and health have increased this year.
The minister announced new allocations for social housing projects.

senses.definition: A person or system that decides how resources, tasks, or data are shared out and given to different users or purposes.
examples: In this company, the finance director acts as the main budget allocator.
In the software, the memory allocator automatically assigns space to new programs.

senses.definition: Plural of allocator: people or systems that decide how to share and give resources, tasks, or data to different users or purposes.
examples: Different memory allocators can affect the performance of the program.
In large organizations, several allocators manage different parts of the budget.

senses.definition: In law, formal permission from a higher court allowing an appeal or the allowance of a legal writ or costs.
examples: The supreme court granted allocatur so the case could be reviewed.
Without allocatur, the party could not proceed with the appeal.

senses.definition: A rare medical condition in which a feeling caused on one side of the body is experienced as if it were on the opposite side.
examples: In allocheiria, a touch on the left hand may be felt on the right hand instead.
The neurologist described allocheiria as a disorder of how the brain locates touch on the body.

senses.definition: Plural of allocheiria: several instances or types of the condition where sensations on one side of the body are felt on the opposite side.
examples: Different allocheirias have been reported in patients with brain lesions.
The article compared visual and tactile allocheirias in detail.

senses.definition: A rare condition in which a sensation is felt at a body part different from the place where the stimulus is actually applied, often on the opposite side.
examples: Allochetia may cause a touch on the foot to be felt on the hand.
The report described allochetia as a form of disturbed sensory localization.

senses.definition: A rare or historical name for a mineral, used mainly in scientific or specialist texts.
examples: Allochetite was listed among several uncommon minerals in the catalogue.
The sample contained traces of allochetite and other related minerals.

senses.definition: A rare neurological condition in which a person feels a stimulus as coming from the wrong place on the body, often on the opposite side.
examples: Allochezia is sometimes described in patients with complex sensory disorders.
The clinician noted allochezia when pinprick sensations were reported on the opposite limb.

senses.definition: Relating to or showing allochiria, with sensations or responses appearing on the side of the body opposite to the stimulus.
examples: Allochiral responses were observed during the neurological examination.
The patient showed an allochiral pattern of sensation in the upper limbs.

senses.definition: In a way that involves allochiria, with sensations or responses occurring on the opposite side of the body from the stimulus.
examples: Touch applied to the left arm was allochirally perceived on the right arm.
The pain was reported allochirally, appearing on the side opposite the injury.

senses.definition: A neurological disorder in which a person reacts to a stimulus on one side of the body as if it were on the opposite side.
examples: In allochiria, a sound heard in the left ear may be reported as coming from the right.
Allochiria is often associated with lesions in the right parietal lobe.

senses.definition: Plural of allochiria: several cases or types of the condition where stimuli are experienced on the opposite side of the body.
examples: Different allochirias have been described in the neurological literature.
The study compared somatosensory and visual allochirias.

senses.definition: A form or type of chlorophyll described in specialist scientific literature.
examples: The researchers isolated allochlorophyll from the plant samples.
Allochlorophyll was mentioned as a minor pigment in the study.

senses.definition: Changing color or showing different colors under different conditions, especially in minerals or chemicals.
examples: Under strong light the crystal appeared allochroic.
Allochroic substances may change color when heated.

senses.definition: A rare mineral name found mainly in specialist geological or mineralogical texts.
examples: Allochroite occurs in certain metamorphic rocks.
The museum has a small specimen of allochroite on display.

senses.definition: Describing a mineral whose color is mainly caused by small amounts of impurities rather than its basic structure.
examples: Many gemstones are allochromatic, so trace elements determine their color.
Quartz can be allochromatic, appearing in different colors depending on impurities.

senses.definition: Showing changes in color under different conditions; color-changing.
examples: The dye was described as allochroous in the early literature.
Allochroous compounds were studied for their optical properties.

senses.definition: A large block of rock that has been moved from its original place, usually along a fault, to lie on top of other rocks.
examples: The mountain range includes a major allochthon that has slid over the older rocks.
Geologists mapped the boundary of the allochthon in detail.

senses.definition: Formed or found in a place different from where it originally came from, especially referring to rocks, sediments, or organisms.
examples: The allochthonous rocks were transported by large-scale faulting.
The lake receives allochthonous organic matter from the surrounding forest.

senses.definition: Relating to or derived from a particular isomer of cinnamic acid, described in specialist chemical literature.
examples: Allocinnamic acid was synthesized under controlled conditions.
The allocinnamic derivative showed different melting points.

senses.definition: A mineral name used mainly in older or specialist geological texts.
examples: Alloclase was identified in the thin section of the rock.
The catalogue listed alloclase among several obscure minerals.

senses.definition: A rare mineral containing cobalt and arsenic, known mainly to specialists.
examples: Alloclasite often appears with other cobalt minerals in hydrothermal veins.
The specimen of alloclasite had a metallic luster.

senses.definition: An obscure or dialect word recorded in older sources, probably referring to a bird or small animal.
examples: The term allocochick appears only in a few historical dictionaries.
She came across the strange word allocochick while reading an old text.

senses.definition: Relating to or derived from a particular form of crotonic acid, used in specialist chemical contexts.
examples: The allocrotonic compound showed distinct spectral lines.
Allocrotonic derivatives were examined in the experiment.

senses.definition: Relating to an organism or feature that is hidden or not easily seen because it resembles something else in its environment.
examples: The insect was allocryptic, blending with the bark of the tree.
Allocryptic coloration makes the animal hard to detect.

senses.definition: A variant spelling of allochthonous, meaning formed or coming from somewhere else than its current location.
examples: The report used the spelling allocthonous for the transported sediments.
Allocthonous material was carried into the basin by rivers.

senses.definition: To make a formal speech or address, especially in a legal or ceremonial setting.
examples: The judge allocuted the defendant before sentencing.
He allocuted briefly, expressing regret for his actions.

senses.definition: A formal speech of advice or warning, often given by a judge or religious leader.
examples: The priest’s allocution focused on mercy and forgiveness.
In his allocution, the judge reminded the jury of their duty.

senses.definition: In law, a formal statement made by a defendant in court, usually before sentencing.
examples: During allocution, the defendant apologized to the victims.
The court gave the accused time for allocution before announcing the sentence.

senses.definition: Plural of allocution: several formal speeches or formal statements made in court.
examples: The pope’s allocutions were widely published at the time.
The judge listened carefully to the defendants’ allocutions.

senses.definition: Relating to allocution or a formal address to someone, especially in a ceremonial or legal context.
examples: The allocutive style of the speech was direct and personal.
Some languages have special allocutive forms that change depending on who is being addressed.

senses.definition: A chemical dye of the cyanine group, used mainly in specialist chemical or photographic contexts.
examples: The solution contained traces of allocyanine.
Allocyanine dyes were once used in early photographic processes.

senses.definition: Land owned completely and freely, without any duty of service to a lord; used in historical and legal texts.
examples: Under that system, a free allod was rare.
The document described his estate as an allod rather than a feudal holding.

senses.definition: A rare mineral name found mainly in specialist mineralogical literature.
examples: Allodelphite was identified using X-ray diffraction.
Only a few localities worldwide are known for allodelphite.

senses.definition: A technical term in geology or crystallography relating to the way parts of a crystal or structure are joined.
examples: The crystal shows allodesmism in its internal bonding pattern.
Allodesmism was discussed in the context of complex silicate structures.

senses.definition: An obsolete or rare form of the verb lodge, meaning to stay somewhere or to put something in a place.
examples: They allodged with friends during their journey.
He allodged the document safely in a chest.

senses.definition: Plural of allodium: lands held in complete and independent ownership, without feudal duties.
examples: In some regions, allodia existed alongside feudal fiefs.
The charter lists several allodia owned by the monastery.

senses.definition: Describing land that is owned completely and independently, without owing service or rent to a feudal lord.
examples: The peasants fought to turn their holdings into allodial land.
Allodial tenure contrasted with feudal obligations.

senses.definition: A legal or political system in which land is held as allodial property, free from feudal duties.
examples: Allodialism became a topic of debate among legal scholars.
The shift toward allodialism changed land relations in the region.

senses.definition: A supporter of allodial land ownership or a person who holds allodial property.
examples: The allodialist opposed the introduction of new feudal dues.
Many independent farmers were described as allodialists.

senses.definition: The quality or state of being allodial, or fully owned without feudal duties.
examples: The allodiality of the estate was questioned in court.
Allodiality became more common as feudal structures weakened.

senses.definition: In an allodial way, with land owned completely and independently.
examples: The land was held allodially, without duties to any lord.
Properties that are owned allodially are rare in some systems.

senses.definition: A person who holds land as an allod, in full ownership without feudal duties.
examples: As an allodian, he owed no service to any lord.
The rights of allodians were carefully recorded in the charter.

senses.definition: Plural of allodiary: people who hold or manage allodial lands.
examples: The allodiaries of the region formed a distinct social group.
Records of allodiaries are limited in the surviving documents.

senses.definition: A person who owns allodial land, or who is recorded as such in legal documents.
examples: The allodiary appears several times in the property register.
As an allodiary, she was free from feudal dues.

senses.definition: Plural of allody or allod, referring to lands held in full free ownership.
examples: The charter lists several allodies belonging to the family.
Over time, some allodies were turned into feudal fiefs.

senses.definition: The process of turning land into allodial property, free from feudal duties.
examples: Allodification of peasant holdings changed the balance of power.
The law allowed the gradual allodification of leased estates.

senses.definition: Land that is owned completely and independently, not held from any lord and free from feudal duties.
examples: The estate was described as an allodium in the record.
Owning an allodium gave the farmer unusual independence.

senses.definition: Plural of allodium: lands held in full and independent ownership without feudal duties.
examples: Few allodiums survived into the later Middle Ages.
The document lists several allodiums and their owners.
